rows.v: The number of strokes an expert golfer is expected to need to complete a hole (or the course). The baseline every score is measured against.
She hit the par-4 green in two and two-putted for a routine par — exactly the score the hole was designed to yield.
From the Latin 'par' (equal), borrowed via the stock-market sense of 'par value' (the standard or face value) into golf around 1900.
A score of one stroke under par on a hole. The bread-and-butter good score for skilled players.
He drained a 15-foot putt for birdie, pumping his fist as the ball dropped one under par.
From early-1900s American slang where 'bird' meant anything excellent. An 'eagle' and 'albatross' later extended the bird theme to bigger feats.
A score of two strokes under par on a hole — a rare and exciting result, usually on a par-5 reached in two.
She reached the par-5 in two and rolled in the putt for eagle, vaulting up the leaderboard.
An extension of the 'birdie' bird theme: if one-under is a birdie, two-under is a bigger bird — an eagle.
A score of one stroke over par on a hole. The most common 'mistake' score for average and good players alike.
He missed the green and couldn't get up and down, settling for a bogey to drop one back.
From 'the Bogey Man' of a popular 1890s song; originally 'bogey' meant the score a steady, mythical opponent ('Colonel Bogey') would make — close to today's par.
A score of three strokes under par on a hole — also called a 'double eagle.' One of the rarest feats in golf.
His second shot on the par-5 rolled into the cup for an albatross — a once-in-a-lifetime three-under.
Continuing the bird theme to its extreme: an albatross is a far larger, rarer bird than an eagle, fitting golf's rarest common score.
A numerical measure of a golfer's ability that lets players of different skill levels compete fairly by adjusting their scores.
With a 12 handicap, she gets to subtract strokes, letting her match against a scratch golfer come down to the wire.
From the old game 'hand-in-cap', a betting practice where stakes were drawn from a cap to even the odds — the root of 'handicap' across all sports.
A hole-in-one — sinking the ball with a single stroke from the tee. Golf's most celebrated stroke of luck and skill.
On the par-3 17th he made an ace, and tradition obliged him to buy drinks for the whole clubhouse afterward.
From the playing-card 'ace' (the single, highest card) via its general sense of 'the best' — a one-stroke hole being the ultimate.
Getting the ball into the hole in two strokes from off the green — typically a chip or pitch followed by one putt.
He missed the green but got up and down from the bunker, his sand shot to three feet saving par.
Descriptive of the two-shot sequence: the ball goes 'up' (the approach onto the green) and 'down' (the putt into the hole).
A long shot played from the tee, usually with a driver, to start a hole and cover maximum distance.
His drive split the fairway 300 yards out, leaving just a short iron into the green.
From the general sense of 'to drive' (to propel forcefully). The tee shot 'drives' the ball the farthest of any stroke in the bag.
A short, low shot played near the green that spends little time in the air and rolls toward the hole like a putt.
She chipped it cleanly off the tight lie, the ball checking once and trickling to a foot from the cup.
From the everyday sense of a small, quick action — a 'chip' is a brief, clipped strike, in contrast to a full swing.
A stroke played on the green with a putter to roll the ball into (or near) the hole. The game's most precise shot.
He left himself a tricky downhill putt and lagged it close, tapping in for his par.
From an old Scottish form of 'put' (to push or place gently). Golf preserves the original Scots spelling and pronunciation ('putt').
A short, lofted shot that flies higher than a chip and stops more quickly, used to approach the green from close range.
From 60 yards she hit a soft pitch that landed by the pin and spun to a stop.
From 'to pitch' (to throw or toss). The shot 'pitches' the ball up into the air on a steep, lofted trajectory.
Two intentional curves: a 'fade' curves gently left-to-right (for a right-hander); a 'draw' curves right-to-left. Controlled shot shapes, not mistakes.
He played a controlled fade around the trees, the ball drifting gently back toward the fairway.
'Fade' from the sense of gradually drifting; 'draw' from the ball being 'drawn' back across its line. Their exaggerated, accidental cousins are the slice and hook.
Two unwanted curves: a 'slice' veers sharply away (left-to-right for a right-hander); a 'hook' veers sharply the other way. The amateur's common miss.
His slice sent the ball curving into the trees, the clubface open at impact carving it hard right.
'Slice' from the cutting, glancing contact that imparts the spin; 'hook' from the sharply curving, hook-like flight path.
A very high, soft shot hit with a wide-open clubface that lands almost vertically and stops fast — used to clear a hazard with little green to work with.
Short-sided with the bunker between him and the pin, he opened the face and hit a delicate flop that landed soft.
From the way the ball 'flops' down almost straight onto the green with minimal roll — a dramatic, risky shot popularized by short-game artists.
A low, controlled shot hit with a shortened swing to keep the ball under wind or tree branches.
Stuck under a low limb, she punched a knockdown shot that stayed low and ran up onto the green.
From 'to punch' (a short, sharp strike). The abbreviated, jabbing swing 'punches' the ball out low and controlled.
The closely-mown strip of grass between tee and green that offers the ideal lie for the next shot.
Hitting the fairway off the tee is half the battle — from the short grass, every approach is easier.
From the nautical 'fairway' (a clear, navigable channel). Golf borrowed it for the clear, well-kept path toward the green.
The longer, thicker grass bordering the fairway that makes shots harder to control. Punishment for a wayward drive.
His drive drifted into the thick rough, the long grass grabbing the clubhead and killing his distance.
From the plain sense of 'rough' (uneven, unkempt ground) — the deliberately untamed grass that contrasts with the manicured fairway.
The smooth, very closely-mown area surrounding the hole, designed for putting. Also called the 'putting green'.
She hit the green in regulation and two-putted, her ball never leaving the short grass.
From the simple description of the well-watered, vivid-green putting surface — the most carefully maintained turf on the course.
A hazard consisting of a depression filled with sand, requiring a specialized sand shot to escape. Often called a 'sand trap'.
He found the greenside bunker but splashed out beautifully, the ball floating up on a cushion of sand.
From the Scots 'bunker' (a seat, chest, or earthen bank). On Scotland's links courses, natural sandy hollows became the original golf bunkers.
The starting area of each hole, where players hit their first shot, usually from a small peg (the 'tee').
Standing on the tee box, she teed it up high and ripped her opening drive down the middle.
From the Scots 'tee', the original small pile of sand (later a wooden peg) the ball was placed on to start a hole.
The flagstick marking the hole's location on the green, so players can see the target from a distance. Also called the 'flag'.
He fired straight at the pin, the ball settling six feet from the hole for a birdie look.
From the simple sense of a slender pin or rod. The flag atop it lets golfers aim at a hole they can't otherwise see from the fairway.
A hole that bends left or right partway down its length, like a dog's hind leg, requiring a shaped or strategically placed drive.
The par-4 doglegs sharply right, tempting big hitters to cut the corner over the trees.
From the resemblance of the angled fairway to the bent hind leg of a dog — a vivid, century-old golf coinage.
The slightly longer grass ringing the green, between the putting surface and the rough. Also called the 'apron' or 'collar'.
Her ball came to rest on the fringe, and she chose to putt from just off the green's edge.
From the everyday 'fringe' (a border or edge). It's the transitional collar of grass framing the smooth green.
The longest-hitting club, with the largest head and lowest loft, used mainly off the tee for maximum distance. Also called the '1-wood'.
He pulled the driver on the long par-5, swinging for the fences to reach the green in two.
Named for its job: it 'drives' the ball the farthest. Modern oversized titanium drivers are a far cry from the wooden originals.
A high-lofted iron designed for short, high shots and getting out of trouble — pitching, sand, lob, and gap wedges each serve a range.
From 90 yards she reached for a wedge, lofting the ball high so it landed soft and stopped quickly.
From the wedge-like shape and steep loft of the clubface, which lifts the ball sharply upward — the most lofted clubs in the bag.
A numbered set of clubs (typically 3 through 9) with flatter, angled faces, used for a wide range of approach shots. Lower numbers hit farther.
He flushed a 7-iron to the heart of the green, the crisp contact sending it right at the flag.
From the iron (now steel) clubheads that distinguished them from the old wooden-headed 'woods'. The numbering reflects increasing loft.
A flat-faced club designed solely for rolling the ball along the green into the hole. The most-used club in any round.
She lined up the putt, brought the putter back smoothly, and rolled it dead center.
Named directly for its single job — to 'putt'. It's the one club a golfer might use on every single hole of a round.
A club blending the features of an iron and a fairway wood — easier to hit than a long iron, with a rounded head. Also called a 'rescue' club.
Replacing his hard-to-hit 3-iron with a hybrid, he found long approaches suddenly far more forgiving.
Named literally for being a 'hybrid' of two club types. The popular brand name 'Rescue' captures its job — bailing players out of long shots.
The angle of a clubface relative to vertical, which determines how high and far the ball flies. More loft means higher, shorter shots.
The lob wedge's huge loft sent the ball nearly straight up, dropping it softly over the bunker.
From the old sense of 'loft' (a height or upper space) — the angle that 'lofts' the ball into the air. Each club's loft is carefully calibrated.
An informal 'do-over' — replaying a bad shot with no penalty. Not allowed under the rules, but common in casual rounds.
After a snap-hook off the first tee, his buddies waved him to take a mulligan and try again.
Popularly traced to a 1920s golfer named David Mulligan, who supposedly took an extra shot — though several origin stories compete and none is certain.
A short putt that the other players agree to count as made without actually being struck — conceded as too easy to miss.
His ball stopped six inches away, and his opponent waved it off as a gimme.
From the casual contraction of 'give me' (the stroke). Strictly, gimmes aren't allowed in stroke play, but they're a friendly staple of casual golf.
A warning shouted to alert other players that a ball is heading their way and they should take cover.
His drive sailed toward the next fairway and he yelled 'Fore!' as loud as he could.
Likely from the military 'beware before!' or a caddie's old cry of 'forecaddie' — a warning to those ahead. The exact origin is debated.
A golfer with a handicap of zero, skilled enough to play to par on average. The benchmark of an excellent amateur.
As a scratch golfer, she gets no handicap strokes — she's expected to shoot around par every time out.
From the old sporting sense of 'scratch' as the starting line from which the best competitors begin, receiving no head start or allowance.
A horribly mis-hit shot struck off the club's heel (hosel), sending the ball squirting sharply sideways. Golf's most dreaded miss.
He shanked it dead right off the hosel, the ball rocketing sideways into the trees — every golfer's nightmare.
From the 'shank' (the neck or hosel of the club) that strikes the ball. So feared that some golfers refuse to even say the word aloud.
Affectionate slang for the clubhouse bar — the 'hole' players head to after finishing the 18 on the course.
After a rough round, the foursome consoled themselves at the 19th hole, swapping stories over drinks.
A playful coinage: a standard course has 18 holes, so the bar where golfers gather afterward becomes the unofficial '19th'.
